method3.generated_article:
==Geography==
Kavachi is a shallow submarine stratovolcano in the southwest Pacific Ocean, about 24 km south of Vangunu Island in the western Solomon Islands.[2][3] It lies within the tectonically active Pacific Ring of Fire and formed at a convergent boundary where the Pacific Plate subducts beneath the Solomon Plate.[2][3] The volcano rises more than 1,200 m from the seafloor, with its summit lying only about 20 m below sea level.[2] Its shallow summit allows eruptions to discolor the surrounding seawater and produce plumes that can be observed from aircraft and satellites.[2][3]

The main summit contains an oblong, pockmarked crater measuring approximately 120 m by 75 m, with a rim at an average depth of about 24 m below sea level.[5] Bathymetric surveys show deepest soundings on the main peak of about 70 m, with surrounding flanks descending at roughly 18° to depths greater than 1,000 m.[5] A secondary summit, described as a southwest extension, lies about 1.3 km southwest of the main summit and is separated from it by a narrow col.[5] The broader volcanic edifice has been described as a roughly conical feature about 8 km in diameter at its base.[4]

Kavachi is notable for frequent phreatomagmatic activity, in which seawater interacts explosively with magma.[5] Its eruptions have periodically built short-lived islands above sea level, which are later eroded by wave action.[3][4] The combination of shallow summit depth, explosive activity, and ephemeral island formation makes Kavachi one of the most active and geographically distinctive submarine volcanoes in the Pacific.[2][5]

==Eruptions==
Kavachi is a highly active shallow submarine volcano, with near-surface eruptions every few years that have repeatedly built temporary islands.[1] Since its first recorded eruption in 1939, new islands have formed at least nine times, but they were generally too small and insufficiently protected by solid lava flows to resist wave erosion and were submerged again within months.[1] A summary based on Global Volcanism Program data reports 33 historic eruptions and notes that the known record is probably incomplete, especially for earlier activity.[1]

Late in 1951, activity at Kavachi was associated with frequent small tsunamis, with one cited source giving 22 December 1951 as a possible date.[1] In May 2000, scientists aboard the CSIRO research vessel *Franklin* observed a new phase of island-building activity after a nine-year dormancy, including ash and incandescent lava blocks ejected up to 70 m above sea level and sulfurous steam plumes rising to about 500 m.[2] During the same episode, the summit was forming a sandy, ashy beach about 2 m below sea level.[2]

Explosive eruptions occurred daily from 27 November to 13 December 2001, and on 13 January 2002 volcanic debris and gas bubbles were observed rising from the volcano with frequent loud noises.[2] During an eruptive cycle reported in November 2003, a 15 m-high island formed, but by the 16th the summit was about 32 m below sea level and no evidence of volcanic activity was observed for the first time in four years.[2] Volcanic activity was again observed on 15 March 2004 after a period of quiescence.[2]

A January 2015 expedition coincided with a rare lull in activity, allowing researchers to enter and survey the active crater and flanks, access that is normally limited by Kavachi’s isolated location and explosive behavior.[3] The 2015 observations documented hydrothermal and gas activity at the summit and identified a secondary peak southwest of the main summit with evidence of diffuse-flow venting.[3]

==Marine life==
Kavachi became widely known for its unusual marine life after camera observations documented animals inside the crater during quiescent periods, contributing to the popular nickname “Sharkcano”.[2][4][5] The recorded fauna included gelatinous zooplankton, reef fishes, and sharks, including the scalloped hammerhead shark (*Sphyrna lewini*) and the silky shark (*Carcharhinus falciformis*).[4] Cameras recorded sharks swimming within the crater and reacting aggressively to bait, showing that large predators can at least temporarily occupy the plume-affected habitat.[4]

The crater waters are warm, acidic, turbid, and chemically enriched by volcanic gases and suspended minerals.[4][5] Measurements reported from the crater area found temperatures up to about 13 °C above surrounding waters, reaching approximately 42 °C, with an estimated pH of about 6.1.[4] The hydrothermal plume contains carbon dioxide- and sulfur-rich gases that may support microbial communities, including *Sulfurimonas* bacteria capable of chemosynthesis using sulfur compounds and carbon dioxide.[4]

It remains uncertain whether sharks live in the crater continuously or move in and out between eruptive episodes.[1][5] Proposed explanations for their presence include behavioural avoidance of dangerous eruptive phases, detection of chemical or seismic warning cues, and physiological tolerance of warmer and more acidic water.[1] The site has therefore been described as a natural laboratory for studying extreme marine ecosystems, marine resilience, and biological responses to rapid changes in ocean chemistry.[1][5]

Kavachi is a shallow submarine stratovolcano in the southwest Pacific Ocean, about 24 km south of Vangunu Island in the western Solomon Islands.[2][3] It lies within the tectonically active Pacific Ring of Fire and formed at a convergent boundary where the Pacific Plate subducts beneath the Solomon Plate.[2][3] The volcano rises more than 1,200 m from the seafloor, with its summit lying only about 20 m below sea level.[2] Its shallow summit allows eruptions to discolor the surrounding seawater and produce plumes that can be observed from aircraft and satellites.[2][3]

Kavachi is notable for frequent phreatomagmatic activity, in which seawater interacts explosively with magma.[5] Its eruptions have periodically built short-lived islands above sea level, which are later eroded by wave action.[3][4] The combination of shallow summit depth, explosive activity, and ephemeral island formation makes Kavachi one of the most active and geographically distinctive submarine volcanoes in the Pacific.[2][5]
